facts: Zakat is the third pillar of Islam and means 'purification' and 'growth' — it purifies wealth and helps it grow through blessing.
Muslims are required to give 2.5% of their qualifying wealth annually to those in need.
Zakat is not charity — it is an obligatory act of worship, considered a right of the poor upon the wealth of the rich.

There are eight categories of people eligible to receive Zakat, as defined in the Quran (9:60): the poor, the needy, Zakat administrators, those whose hearts need winning over, freeing captives, those in debt, in the cause of Allah, and travelers in need.
The nisab (minimum threshold) for Zakat on gold is approximately 85 grams of gold or its cash equivalent.
Zakat was mandated in the second year after the Prophet's ﷺ migration to Madinah.
In the early Islamic state, Zakat was collected by the government and distributed to the eight eligible categories — functioning as one of history's earliest social welfare systems.
Zakat al-Fitr is a special charity given at the end of Ramadan before the Eid prayer, so that even the poorest can celebrate Eid.
The total global Zakat pool is estimated at $200–$600 billion annually, making it one of the largest forms of wealth redistribution in the world.
Prophet Muhammad ﷺ said that wealth does not decrease by giving charity — a principle that encourages generosity.
Sadaqah (voluntary charity) goes beyond Zakat and can be given at any time in any amount, including non-monetary acts of kindness.
In many Muslim-majority countries, Zakat is collected institutionally and used to fund education, healthcare, and poverty alleviation.
Zakat on agricultural produce varies: 10% for rain-fed crops and 5% for irrigated crops.
Zakat is also applicable on livestock, trade goods, and certain investments — not just cash savings.
The Quran mentions Zakat 30 times, often paired with Salah, emphasizing that worship of God and care for others are inseparable.
Refusing to pay Zakat is considered a major sin in Islam, and the first Caliph Abu Bakr even declared war on those who refused.
Modern scholars have developed guidelines for Zakat on stocks, cryptocurrency, retirement accounts, and other contemporary financial instruments.
Waqf (endowment) is a related Islamic concept where assets are donated permanently for charitable purposes — many historic universities and hospitals were funded this way.
reflections: How does giving Zakat change your relationship with your wealth and material possessions?
The Quran says Zakat 'purifies' wealth. What do you think this means spiritually?
In what ways can you go beyond obligatory Zakat to practice generosity (sadaqah) in daily life?
How does Zakat serve as a bridge between worship of God and service to humanity?
Reflect on the concept that the poor have a right to a portion of your wealth. How does this change your perspective on giving?
What impact would it have on global poverty if every eligible Muslim paid their full Zakat?
How can you ensure your Zakat reaches those who need it most in your community?
Beyond money, what other forms of charity can you give? Time, skills, a kind word?
How does the practice of Zakat help combat greed and materialism in your life?
Reflect on a time when giving — whether Zakat or sadaqah — brought you unexpected joy or blessing.
